turns.content: we're sitting at minus 2 today. mid April. that is not right.
Minus 2 in mid-April is definitely warm for your area. What would you normally expect this time of year?
minus 15 to minus 20 still. we've been above minus 10 for almost a week. the snow is getting soft on top. you can feel it when you walk. it shouldn't be doing that yet.

A week of sustained warmth like that in April is significant. When the snow surface starts softening and losing its structure, it changes travel conditions, ice stability, melt timing — everything. Is it affecting your ability to get out on the land?

the trails are usable in the morning when it refreezes overnight. by afternoon it's soft. the machine bogs down in it. another week of this and some of the routes I use won't be safe. especially near water.

That freeze-thaw cycle is tricky — the overnight refreeze gives you a window but it's shrinking every day. Near water is the real concern. Warm temperatures work the ice from above while the water is already working it from below. Things can go from stable to dangerous quickly.

Are you restricting your travel to mornings now, or pulling back from certain routes entirely?

go out early. back by noon. that's the safe window. any lake crossings I'm avoiding unless I've checked the ice myself that day. some of the younger guys don't think about it. they see the surface and figure it's fine.

Morning-only travel and personally verifying ice before crossing is the right call. Surface appearance can be completely deceiving during a warm spell — ice can look solid on top but be honeycombing from below.

The younger guys running on assumptions is concerning. Even a few days of above-normal temperatures can take ice from safe to dangerous. Have you been able to pass the word around about conditions?

I told a few. they nod but they still go out in the afternoon. nothing teaches you like going through the ice once. problem is going through once can kill you. you don't always get to learn the lesson twice.

That's the hard truth. Ice doesn't give second chances consistently. All you can do is share what you know and hope some of it sticks.

Do you have a sense of whether this warm spell is going to hold, or is colder weather coming back?

forecast shows above minus 10 for the next 10 days. if that holds then breakup is going to come early. maybe 2 or 3 weeks early. that changes everything. when you can travel. where you can go. when the water opens up. the whole schedule shifts.

Two to three weeks early breakup is a major shift. It compresses your late-winter travel season and changes timing for fieldwork, hunting, supply runs — everything that depends on snow and ice. Early breakup also means a longer mud season before the ground firms up for summer travel.

Are you having to reschedule things because of this?

a few things I wanted to do on the land in late April might not happen. that's ok. you plan around the weather. the weather doesn't plan around you. been that way forever. it's just harder to predict what the weather is going to do these days.

That's well put. Adapting to conditions is nothing new, but when the patterns you've relied on for decades start shifting, the baseline keeps moving. That's a different kind of challenge than dealing with one unusual year.
yeah. anyway. just keeping an eye on it. morning travel only for now. we'll see what the next week brings.
Sounds like the right approach — mornings only and stay off the questionable crossings. Stay safe out there, and hopefully the forecast is wrong about that 10-day stretch.
